text: This video demonstrates an operation of robotic-assisted right haematolectomy with intracorporeal anastomosis.
This video demonstrates an operation of robotic-assisted right haematolectomy with intracorporeal anastomosis.
This is a seventy-eight-year-old female with histology-proven adenocarcinoma in the cecum.

The video shows the placement of robotic ports on the patient's abdomen, which are used for the robotic-assisted right hemicolectomy procedure.

The surgical setup includes an 8mm suprapubic port, a 5mm port in the right iliac fossa, and additional 12mm ports.
The video shows the placement of surgical ports for a robotic-assisted right hemicolectomy: an 8mm suprapubic port, a 5mm port in the right iliac fossa, and additional 12mm ports.
The laparoscopic view shows the initial assessment with the patient positioned head tilt and right side up, exposing the duodenum.

The surgeon isolates the ileocolic pedicle and secures it using a laparoscopic stapler, ensuring control over the vascular supply.
The surgeon isolates the ileocolic pedicle and secures it using a laparoscopic stapler, preparing for resection during the robotic-assisted right hemicolectomy.
The surgeon performs a standard medial to lateral dissection, providing a clear view of the duodenum during the procedure.

The surgeon uses a single fenestrated grasper with the left hand, visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealing device in the right hand, connected to bipolar diathermy, to seal vessels during the colectomy procedure.

The surgeon continues the dissection towards the lateral sidewall, mobilizing the hepatic flexure using laparoscopic instruments.

The surgeon identifies the site of transection in the transverse colon after controlling the right branch of the midocolic vessels, preparing for the next step in the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies the site of transection in the transverse colon after controlling the right branch of the midocolic vessels, preparing for the next step in the procedure.
The surgeon uses a robotic linear stapler to divide the transverse colon at the identified transection site, completing the separation of the bound tissue.
The surgeon uses a robotic linear stapler to divide the bound tissue, completing the transection in the transverse colon.
The surgeon performs a transection in the terminal ileum using robotic instruments, with visible separation of the intestinal tissue.
The surgeon performs a transection in the terminal ileum using robotic instruments, following previous dissection and mobilization steps.
The surgeon places the transected bowel above the liver, preparing it for removal at the end of the colectomy procedure.
The transected bowel is positioned above the liver, prepared for removal at the conclusion of the procedure.
The surgeon places a stay suture at the site in preparation for a site-to-site anastomosis, securing the tissue for the upcoming connection between bowel segments.
The surgeon places a stay suture in preparation for a site-to-site anastomosis, securing the tissue at the intended connection point.

A laparoscopic burdock clamp is applied to the proximal small bowel to prevent spillage of its contents. Incisions are made in the transverse colon and small bowel, followed by the insertion of a robotic linear stapler.

The surgeon performs a two-layer anastomosis, with the inner layer using continuous sutures and the outer layer using interrupted sutures, joining the transected bowel segments.
The surgeon performs a two-layer anastomosis, with the inner layer being sutured continuously and the outer layer using interrupted sutures.
The surgeon uses the endo wrist feature of the robotic system to suture with both hands, demonstrating the convenience and dexterity provided by the instrument.
The surgeon uses the endo wrist feature of the robotic system to suture with both hands, demonstrating the convenience and dexterity provided by the instrument.
The surgeon applies interrupted sutures to the second layer of the anastomosis, completing the two-layer closure of the bowel.
The surgeon applies interrupted sutures to the second layer of the anastomosis, completing the outer layer of the bowel connection.
The surgeon removes the laparoscopic buttock clamp from the proximal small bowel, which was previously applied to prevent spillage during the procedure.
The surgeon removes the laparoscopic buttock clamp from the surgical site.
The surgeon removes the bowel through a slightly extended umbilical port using a wound protector. The video shows the appearance of the abdomen immediately after the surgery.
